sentence: The patient presents with a follow-up for acne, stating that their condition is improving.

They report using the prescribed medications as directed, including clindamycin lotion, tretinoin cream 4-5%, benzoyl peroxide wash for the face, and 5-10% for the chest and back, and sunscreen/moisturizer.
They deny any redness, peeling, dryness, or irritation with use of these medications.
They mention that they have not put much effort into washing their chest and back with a higher strength benzoyl peroxide wash as recommended.
They report feeling satisfied with the improvement of their acne on the face but mention a mild flare on the back.
Physical exam: The patient has scattered inflammatory papules and pustules across the upper back and posterior shoulders, with less than five inflammatory papules on the forehead, cheeks, and chin.
Assessment: The patient's acne vulgaris is improved and well-controlled on the face but shows a mild flare on the back.
Problem list: Acne vulgaris face active; Acne vulgaris back active; [NOT MENTIONED]
I prescribed Panoxyl 10% foaming wash to be used daily in the shower on the back.
I recommended continuing the current treatment plan for the face.
I will send a new prescription for clindamycin with 11 refills and a new prescription for tretinoin cream with 3 refills.
I provided the patient with samples of Panoxyl 10% and coupons.
I will see the patient back in one year for their medicine refill.
